summary: Martin recounts his formative 1967 experience watching someone program an ECP-18 computer, which set his life's course toward programming. Through correspondence with another early programmer, he discovers Judy Allen, a remarkable woman who wrote a two-pass assembler in binary machine language while managing a household. Martin reflects on how programming was once 'women's work' - from Ada Lovelace through Grace Hopper - until the late 1960s when society recognized that computers manipulated symbols, not just numbers, conferring real power. Once programming became associated with power, it became 'man's work' and women were driven out. He ends questioning whether the technician who inspired him might have been a woman like Judy Allen.
key_points: Programming was historically considered 'women's work' - drudgery beneath men who built hardware - from Ada Lovelace through the 1960s
Women like Annie Jump Cannon, Grace Hopper, and Judy Allen did groundbreaking work in computing when it was undervalued
The shift occurred in the late 1960s when popular culture (Star Trek, 2001) revealed computers as symbol manipulators with boundless power
Once programming was perceived as powerful rather than clerical, men invaded the field and drove women out
Martin's own certainty that his inspiration was male reflects the cultural assumption that power must come from men
money_quotes: This woman was Bad ASS! She wrote a M_____ F____ two-pass assembler, in binary machine language, in 1024 words, in a couple of months, using nothing but the front panel switches.
To some extent programming was considered to be Women's Work.
Computing was Women's Work. Indeed, they called the women: Computers.
These machines manipulated symbols. And if you can manipulate symbols, you can do anything! If you can manipulate symbols, you have power!
Power has a gender, and that gender is Male.
When programmers became powerful, programming became Man's Work.
You couldn't absorb that kind of power from a woman, could you?
key_insight: Programming shifted from 'women's work' to 'men's work' precisely when society recognized that manipulating symbols meant wielding power.
